quiz.title: Unit 10 Quiz 3: Phillips Curve Deep Dive
quiz.subtitle: Expectations, Potential GDP, Supply Shocks, and Policy Responses
quiz.subject: Macroeconomics
quiz.topic: First Year - SRPC Shifts, LRPC, Potential GDP, Cost-Push vs Demand-Pull, Bank of Canada Policy

questions.question:
Premise 1: The domestic economy is currently overheated, with unemployment below the natural rate.
Premise 2: Workers and firms observe rising price levels and adjust their expectations, anticipating that inflation will continue to accelerate.

Which of the following statements is/are FALSE regarding the short-run effects of this increase in inflation expectations? (Select all that apply.)
options: A. The short-run Phillips curve (SRPC) shifts upward, reflecting higher inflation expectations built into wage and price contracts.
B. To preserve real wages, labor unions and individual workers will negotiate for higher nominal wage increases.
C. The economy will move further up and to the left along the original, stationary short-run Phillips curve.
D. The increase in expected inflation can lead to stagflationary pressures, where the inflation rate rises even if the unemployment rate begins to increase.
E. The actual inflation rate will instantly jump by the exact magnitude of the change in expectations, keeping the real interest rate constant.

F. If the central bank does not intervene, the combination of high actual inflation and rising expected inflation will likely keep the economy away from the long-run equilibrium for an extended period.
questions.explanation:
C is false: An increase in expected inflation shifts the SRPC. Movement along the curve only occurs due to changes in Aggregate Demand.

E is false: Actual inflation does not adjust 'instantly' or in an exact 1:1 ratio due to nominal rigidities (sticky prices and long-term contracts).

questions.question: Which of the following is TRUE when an economy is producing at its potential GDP? (Select all that apply.)
options: A. The unemployment rate equals the natural rate of unemployment.
B. The economy is producing as much as it possibly can, with every factory running 24/7 and every worker working overtime.
C. The actual inflation rate equals what workers and firms expected it to be.
D. There is no cyclical unemployment.
E. The short-run Phillips curve crosses the long-run Phillips curve.
F. There is no structural and frictional unemployment.
questions.explanation:
A is true: Potential GDP means the economy is producing at the natural rate of unemployment.

C is true: According to the Phillips curve equation, when unemployment equals the natural rate, actual inflation equals expected inflation.

D is true: Cyclical unemployment is zero at potential GDP because the economy is neither in a boom nor a recession.

E is true: The long-run Phillips curve is vertical at the natural rate. The short-run Phillips curve crosses it at that point.

B is false: That describes an overheated economy above potential GDP. Potential GDP is sustainable, not absolute maximum.

F is false: Structural and frictional unemployment always exist. Together they make up the natural rate. Only cyclical unemployment is zero at potential GDP.

questions.question: An economy is at potential GDP when it experiences an increase in costs. The economy experiences _______.
options: A. a rise in the price level and a decrease in potential GDP
B. a rise in the price level and an increase in real GDP
C. a fall in the price level and a decrease in real GDP
D. stagflation
questions.explanation: An increase in costs (supply shock) shifts Aggregate Supply left. Prices rise and real GDP falls below potential. This combination of rising prices and falling output (rising unemployment) is called stagflation.

questions.question:
When production costs increase and the Bank of Canada seeks to return the economy to full employment, the Bank responds by ________ the quantity of money.
If the Bank of Canada continually responds to successive increases in costs in this manner, a ________ inflation evolves.
options: A. decreasing; cost-push
B. decreasing; demand-pull
C. increasing; demand-pull
D. increasing; cost-push
questions.explanation: To fight unemployment from a supply shock, the Bank must increase the money supply. When done repeatedly, it creates a wage-price spiral driven by cost pressures — cost-push inflation.

questions.question: Along the long-run Phillips curve, _______.
options: A. a fall in the inflation rate brings a higher unemployment rate
B. an increase in the unemployment rate brings a lower inflation rate
C. the unemployment rate is constant at the natural unemployment rate
D. the inflation rate is constant
questions.explanation: The LRPC is vertical at the natural rate of unemployment. No matter the inflation rate, unemployment returns to the natural rate in the long run.

questions.question: If the natural unemployment rate increases and the expected inflation rate remains constant, then _______.
options: A. the short-run Phillips curve shifts rightward and the long-run Phillips curve does not shift
B. the long-run Phillips curve shifts rightward and the short-run Phillips curve shifts rightward
C. the long-run Phillips curve shifts rightward and the short-run Phillips curve does not shift
D. neither the long-run Phillips curve nor the short-run Phillips curve shifts
questions.explanation: The LRPC shifts right because the natural rate increased. The SRPC is anchored by the LRPC, so it also shifts right.

questions.question: If the expected inflation rate increases and the natural rate of unemployment remains constant, then _______.
options: A. neither the long-run Phillips curve nor the short-run Phillips curve shifts
B. the long-run Phillips curve shifts rightward and the short-run Phillips curve does not shift
C. the short-run Phillips curve shifts upward and the long-run Phillips curve does not shift
D. the long-run Phillips curve shifts rightward and the short-run Phillips curve shifts rightward
questions.explanation: Expected inflation shifts only the SRPC (upward). The LRPC is determined solely by the natural rate, which hasn't changed.

questions.question: The short-run Phillips curve suggests a trade-off between:
options: A. Inflation and interest rates
B. Unemployment and GDP growth
C. Inflation and unemployment
D. Taxes and government spending
questions.explanation: The SRPC shows an inverse relationship between inflation and unemployment in the short run.

questions.question: If the central bank increases the money supply unexpectedly, in the short run:
options: A. Unemployment rises and inflation falls
B. Both unemployment and inflation fall
C. Both unemployment and inflation rise
D. Unemployment falls and inflation rises
questions.explanation: Unexpected expansionary policy increases AD, moving the economy up along the SRPC to lower unemployment and higher inflation.

questions.question: A positive supply shock that lowers production costs will:
options: A. Shift the SRPC leftward
B. Shift the SRPC rightward
C. Cause a movement up the SRPC
D. Cause a movement down the SRPC
questions.explanation: Lower costs reduce inflation at every unemployment rate, shifting the SRPC left (or downward).

questions.question: According to the short-run Phillips curve, policymakers can permanently reduce unemployment by:
options: A. Accepting higher inflation
B. Using contractionary policy
C. No — only temporarily
D. Reducing the money supply growth rate
questions.explanation: Any unemployment below the natural rate requires ever-rising inflation if attempted permanently. The trade-off is short-run only.

questions.question: If actual inflation is higher than expected inflation, the unemployment rate will be:
options: A. Above the natural rate
B. Equal to the natural rate
C. Below the natural rate
D. Unchanged regardless of expectations
questions.explanation: When actual > expected, workers are surprised, real wages fall, firms hire more, and unemployment drops below the natural rate.

questions.question: The slope of the short-run Phillips curve becomes steeper if:
options: A. Wages are more flexible
B. Inflation expectations are anchored
C. Wages are very sticky
D. The natural rate rises sharply
questions.explanation: More wage flexibility means prices affect real wages less, so unemployment changes little for a given inflation change — a steeper SRPC.

questions.question: A rise in the natural rate of unemployment will:
options: A. Shift the long-run Phillips curve right but not affect SRPC position
B. Shift both the LRPC and SRPC right
C. Shift only the SRPC left
D. Cause movement along the SRPC
questions.explanation: A higher natural rate shifts the LRPC right. The SRPC also shifts right because the vertical LRPC anchors the SRPC at a higher unemployment rate.

questions.question: In the short-run Phillips curve model, expected inflation is treated as:
options: A. A determinant of the curve's position
B. The only factor causing unemployment
C. Irrelevant for actual inflation
D. Fixed by government decree
questions.explanation: Expected inflation determines where the SRPC is located. Higher expected inflation shifts the curve upward/rightward.

questions.question: Which of the following would cause a movement along the short-run Phillips curve?
options: A. A change in expected inflation
B. A supply shock
C. A change in aggregate demand
D. A rise in the natural rate of unemployment
questions.explanation: Changes in AD move the economy along the existing SRPC. Supply shocks or expectation changes shift the curve itself.

questions.question: The short-run Phillips curve is downward sloping because:
options: A. Wages are perfectly flexible
B. People anticipate all policy changes
C. Nominal wages adjust slowly to price changes
D. Aggregate supply is vertical
questions.explanation: Sticky nominal wages mean higher prices reduce real wages, leading firms to hire more and lower unemployment.

questions.question: A reduction in aggregate demand will, according to the SRPC:
options: A. Raise both inflation and unemployment
B. Lower both inflation and unemployment
C. Raise unemployment but lower inflation
D. Lower unemployment but raise inflation
questions.explanation: Contractionary AD reduces inflation and raises unemployment — a move down along the SRPC.

questions.question: If the public forms adaptive expectations, an unexpected increase in aggregate demand will initially:
options: A. Leave unemployment unchanged
B. Raise unemployment and lower inflation
C. Lower unemployment and raise inflation
D. Lower both unemployment and inflation
questions.explanation: With adaptive expectations, people base forecasts on the past. An unexpected AD increase surprises them, lowering unemployment while raising inflation.

questions.question: The long-run Phillips curve implies that expansionary monetary policy in the long run will:
options: A. Lower unemployment permanently
B. Raise unemployment permanently
C. Raise the inflation rate only
D. Lower the inflation rate only
questions.explanation: In the long run, unemployment returns to the natural rate. Expansionary policy only leads to higher inflation, not lower unemployment.

scoreMessages.90: Excellent! You understand SRPC/LRPC, expectations, supply shocks, and policy responses.
scoreMessages.70: Good work. Review the difference between shifts (expectations, natural rate) and movements (AD changes).
scoreMessages.50: Solid effort. Focus on why the LRPC is vertical and what potential GDP means.
scoreMessages.0: Keep studying. Remember: SRPC slopes down (trade-off). LRPC is vertical at natural rate. Expectations shift SRPC. Natural rate shifts both.
